flashcards.front: What is force?
flashcards.back: A push or pull that changes or tends to change the state of motion or shape of an object.

flashcards.front: What are balanced forces?
flashcards.back: Forces equal in magnitude and opposite in direction that do not change the state of rest or motion of an object.

flashcards.front: What are unbalanced forces?
flashcards.back: Forces that are unequal in magnitude and opposite in direction, causing a change in the state of motion of an object.

flashcards.front: State Newton’s First Law of Motion.
flashcards.back: An object remains at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force.

flashcards.front: What is inertia?
flashcards.back: The natural tendency of an object to resist a change in its state of rest or motion.

flashcards.front: How is inertia related to mass?
flashcards.back: The mass of an object is a measure of its inertia; heavier objects have greater inertia.

flashcards.front: State Newton’s Second Law of Motion.
flashcards.back: The rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of the force.

flashcards.front: Define momentum.
flashcards.back: Momentum (p) is the product of mass (m) and velocity (v) of an object, p = mv.

flashcards.front: What is the SI unit of force?
flashcards.back: Newton (N), defined as the force that produces an acceleration of 1 m/s² in a mass of 1 kg.

flashcards.front: State Newton’s Third Law of Motion.
flashcards.back: To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction, acting on two different bodies.

flashcards.front: What is the SI unit of momentum?
flashcards.back: Kilogram-metre per second (kg·m/s).

flashcards.front: What is the mathematical form of Newton’s Second Law?
flashcards.back: F = ma, where F is force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

flashcards.front: Why do passengers fall forward when a moving bus stops suddenly?
flashcards.back: Because of inertia, their bodies tend to continue moving forward even though the bus has stopped.

flashcards.front: Why do passengers fall backward when a bus starts suddenly?
flashcards.back: Because of inertia, their bodies tend to remain at rest while the bus moves forward.

flashcards.front: Why do leaves fall off when a tree branch is shaken vigorously?
flashcards.back: Due to inertia, leaves resist the sudden motion of the branch and get detached.

flashcards.front: Why does a fielder pull his hands backward while catching a fast ball?
flashcards.back: To increase the time of impact, reducing the rate of change of momentum and hence the force exerted on his hands.

flashcards.front: Why does a gun recoil when fired?
flashcards.back: Because the bullet exerts an equal and opposite force on the gun, causing it to move backward.

flashcards.front: Why do athletes land on sand or cushioned beds in high jump events?
flashcards.back: To increase the time of impact, reducing the force experienced by their bodies.

flashcards.front: Why is it easier to push an empty box than a box full of books?
flashcards.back: Because the box with books has more mass and therefore greater inertia.

flashcards.front: Why does a sailor’s boat move backward when he jumps forward?
flashcards.back: Due to Newton’s Third Law, the forward action force on the sailor produces an equal and opposite reaction force on the boat.
